ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers tuna salad as a closest parent; no specific entry for tuna flakes with mayonnaise dressing exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers prepared/preserved tuna; no specific code for tuna-mayo salad composite dish exists. Used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for tuna mayo salad as a composite prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate of tuna flakes mixed with mayonnaise over a bed of salad vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna salad (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 35188) and Mayonnaise, regular (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 04025); composite estimate weighted for typical tuna-mayo salad formulation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'tuna flakes mayo salad' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tuna salad (NDB 35188), which represents canned tuna mixed with mayonnaise and celery — the closest approved analogue. Per-100g values are taken directly from this entry. Sodium reflects the USDA SR Legacy value and may be slightly lower than restaurant-prepared versions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; tuna (canned in water) and mayonnaise-based salad analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tuna flakes mayo salad. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tuna (canned, GI ~32) and the negligible glycemic contribution of mayonnaise (GI ~0 due to near-zero carbohydrate content). The composite dish is predominantly protein and fat with very low carbohydrate (4 g per 100 g), yielding a low GI and a glycemic load of approximately 2.6 per 200 g serving (GL = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 32/100 × 8.0 = 2.56, rounded to 2.6). Classification: low GI (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from canned tuna and mayonnaise
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 684 mg sodium (~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium tuna or limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and only 4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, tuna flakes mayo salad has a minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI meal option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from tuna versus saturated fat and sodium from mayonnaise
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tuna provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), but the mayonnaise component adds saturated fat and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider using reduced-fat or olive-oil-based mayonnaise and monitoring portion size to balance the cardiovascular benefits of tuna against the lipid and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna Flakes Mayo Salad is a cold prepared dish consisting of canned or poached tuna flakes mixed with mayonnaise and served over or alongside fresh salad vegetables such as lettuce, cucumber, and tomato. It is a popular light meal or side dish found across Malaysian and Singaporean casual dining and café menus, including Western-influenced bistros. The dish is assembled without cooking — the tuna is pre-cooked through the canning process and the mayonnaise is a cold emulsified dressing. The texture is creamy and moist from the mayonnaise, with a flaky, tender tuna body and crisp fresh vegetables providing contrast. The flavour profile is mildly savoury and rich, with the umami of tuna balanced by the tangy creaminess of mayonnaise. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.6 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~12.4 g per 100 g, predominantly unsaturated), and very low in carbohydrates (~4 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI ~32). It is a good source of vitamin B12, niacin, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids from the tuna. The sodium content is moderate to high due to canned tuna and mayonnaise, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when certified halal tuna and mayonnaise are used. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Common allergens include fish and eggs (from mayonnaise). This dish is clinically relevant as a high-protein, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking weight management support.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for tuna flakes mayo salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tuna salad entry (NDB 35188), which is the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogues. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA SR Legacy tuna salad entry is a well-characterised composite that closely matches the dish composition.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 35188 (tuna salad), which includes canned tuna, mayonnaise, and celery. This is the closest approved analogue available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tuna salad entry; tuna does contain vitamin D but the composite salad value is not separately reported. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Vegetables' to 'Fish and seafood', which accurately reflects the primary protein component of this dish. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for canned tuna; the low carbohydrate content of the dish means the glycemic load per 200 g serving is approximately 2.6, which is clinically negligible. 6. Sodium content may be higher in restaurant-prepared versions depending on the brand of canned tuna and mayonnaise used. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy NDB 35188. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tuna salad composite entry.
